Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Pentti Haikonen

b. 1948

Phenomenal engineering: designing the internal appearance of information.

Haikonen belongs to a relatively small but important line of late twentieth- and early twenty-first-century thinkers who treat artificial consciousness not merely as a science-fiction possibility or a philosophical puzzle, but as an engineering target. His position arises after several major stages in philosophy of mind.

The first background is the classical mind-body problem: how mental life, especially subjective experience, relates to the physical body. Haikonen repeatedly frames his own problem in these terms: neural or physical activity is not perceived by us as firings, voltages, molecules, or energy transfer, but as colors, sounds, pains, objects, bodily feelings, meanings, images, and thoughts. For him, the fundamental question is not simply how a system processes information, but why and how processed information becomes internally apparent to the system.

The second background is behaviorism and functionalism. Functionalism, in its standard philosophical form, defines mental states by their causal or functional roles rather than by their material constitution. Haikonen accepts part of this functionalist inheritance: he thinks architectures, causal loops, integration, memory, attention, report, and sensorimotor organization matter. But he rejects the idea that functional performance by itself is enough. A system that behaves intelligently, passes tests, produces reports, or simulates emotion may still lack consciousness if it has no phenomenal internal appearances. In this respect he is closer to anti-behaviorist and anti-reductive qualia theorists than to orthodox computational functionalists.

The third background is the critique of symbolic artificial intelligence. Searle’s Chinese Room argument made famous the objection that syntax is not semantics: a system can manipulate symbols according to rules without understanding them. Harnad’s symbol grounding problem similarly asks how formal symbols can have intrinsic meaning rather than merely meaning assigned by an external interpreter. Haikonen’s theory is deeply shaped by this problem. His answer is that meaning begins with self-explanatory sensory information: colors, sounds, tactile patterns, pains, directions, shapes, and other directly appearing qualities do not first need symbolic interpretation. They are not arbitrary tokens waiting for a dictionary; they are the primary presentation of the world to the system. Words and symbols then acquire meaning by association with these direct qualitative presentations.

The fourth background is the modern debate over qualia and the hard problem of consciousness. Qualia are usually understood as the subjective, phenomenal aspects of experience: what pain feels like, what red looks like, what sound is like from the inside. The hard problem, associated especially with Chalmers, asks why physical or functional processes are accompanied by experience at all rather than occurring unconsciously. Haikonen’s originality is that he refuses to bypass qualia when discussing artificial intelligence. He does not say: build the right behavior and consciousness will follow. He says: no qualia, no genuine percepts; no percepts, no consciousness.

The fifth background is machine consciousness research. By the 2000s and 2010s, machine consciousness became an identifiable interdisciplinary field drawing on cognitive robotics, computational neuroscience, philosophy of mind, global workspace theory, recurrent processing, higher-order theory, attention, and embodiment. Reggia’s survey treats machine consciousness as a field of computational modeling, while more recent theory-based work such as Butlin and colleagues’ 2023 report evaluates AI consciousness through neuroscientific indicators including recurrent processing, global workspace theory, higher-order theory, predictive processing, and attention-schema theory. Haikonen is historically significant because he enters this field from the engineering side while insisting, unusually strongly, that the decisive issue is phenomenal consciousness, not merely access consciousness, behavioral intelligence, or task competence.

So, historically, Haikonen occupies a distinctive middle position. Against dualists, he thinks conscious machines are possible. Against mainstream computationalists, he denies that ordinary digital symbol processing is enough. Against behaviorists and simple functionalists, he insists that subjective appearance is indispensable. Against Searlean anti-AI skepticism, he argues that machines can be conscious if they are built with the right perceptual, associative, embodied, and self-grounding architecture. Against purely metaphysical philosophers, he insists that the problem must eventually be treated as an engineering problem.

2. The development of Haikonen’s project

Haikonen’s intellectual trajectory moves from associative neural architectures to a full theory of machine consciousness. His 1999 doctoral work is described as one of the early dissertations in machine consciousness and introduces the Haikonen associative neuron and a cognitive architecture intended to emulate aspects of human cognition, including perception, attention, associative learning, inner imagery, inner speech, pain and pleasure, introspection, and reportability.

His 2003 book, The Cognitive Approach to Conscious Machines, develops the central alternative to both traditional artificial intelligence and classical neural-network modeling. It proposes that conscious machines should emulate cognitive processes such as inner speech, imagery, emotions, and distributed meaning-bearing representations rather than merely compute formal symbols or numerical functions. The book already contains the central claim that a sufficiently designed machine could be conscious, aware of its own existence, and aware of its own mental content.

His later work sharpens this into a qualia-centered position. In “Qualia and Conscious Machines,” Haikonen argues that qualia are the primary manifestation of sensory information inside the system, not properties of the external world itself. He also argues that human-like consciousness requires qualia, and that true conscious machines must perceive both the world and themselves via qualitative appearances.

In Consciousness and Robot Sentience, Haikonen states the central mind-body problem in a compressed way: consciousness is not explained by ordinary laws of matter and energy, but conscious experience can nevertheless be achieved by systems made of matter and energy. This yields his system-level thesis: conscious experience is a property of certain perceptual systems. The same work ties machine consciousness to direct perception, associative neural processing, sensorimotor integration, information integration, emotional significance, and a self-grounded robot architecture.

His article “Consciousness and Sentient Robots” formulates the doctrine concisely: consciousness is a “subjective internal appearance of information”; explaining consciousness means explaining how this subjective internal appearance arises; and a conscious robot must have phenomenal internal appearances, not merely cognitive abilities.

His later writings on AI and consciousness connect the project explicitly to symbol grounding. He argues that contemporary computers manipulate symbols without intrinsic meanings, that the grounding problem is therefore unavoidable, and that the relevant solution is self-explanatory information of the kind humans experience as qualia. Reportable qualia, on this view, are phenomenal consciousness; machines that genuinely use such self-explanatory information would be conscious in the relevant sense.

The arc is therefore clear: Haikonen begins with an architecture for associative cognitive machines and gradually articulates the philosophy required by that architecture. His mature view is not simply “robots can be conscious.” It is: robots can be conscious only if they possess non-symbolic, directly appearing, self-explanatory qualitative information integrated with perception, action, self-modeling, emotion, memory, and report.

3. The core metaphysics: consciousness as internal appearance

Haikonen’s metaphysical position is subtle and easy to misclassify. He is not a Cartesian substance dualist: he does not posit an immaterial soul added to the machine. He explicitly treats machine consciousness as possible in systems made of ordinary matter and energy. But he is not a reductive materialist in the simple sense either, because he insists that consciousness is not itself matter or energy and is not captured by the ordinary physical description of neural activity. His position is best described as naturalistic, system-level, qualia realist, and anti-eliminativist.

The key term is “appearance.” For Haikonen, conscious information does not merely exist as a code, state vector, data structure, or causal disposition. It appears. It is present to the system as something: red, pain, direction, sound, resistance, bodily contact, object, word, image, expectation, novelty, pleasure, or threat. The physical machinery may be electrical, neural, or artificial, but consciousness is the internal manifestation of that machinery’s information.

This makes Haikonen a realist about phenomenology. He does not reduce qualia to verbal reports or to external behavior. He also does not treat qualia as optional ornaments attached to cognition after the real computational work is done. Qualia are the very way perceptual information becomes available as perceptual information. Hence the slogan implicit throughout his mature work: no qualia, no percepts; no percepts, no consciousness.

At the same time, he does not make qualia mysterious in the sense of supernatural. His proposed explanation is architectural and system-theoretic. Qualia arise in particular kinds of perceptual systems: systems with direct sensory input, associative processing, distributed representations, feedback loops, integration across modalities, attention, memory, sensorimotor grounding, and internal report. The metaphysical burden is shifted from “what substance is mind?” to “what kind of system allows information to appear subjectively?”

A useful reconstruction is this:

Consciousness, for Haikonen, is not a thing over and above the functioning system. It is also not identical with any one physical component. It is a mode of manifestation of information in a sufficiently integrated perceptual-cognitive system. Its existence depends on physical processes, but its description is not exhausted by the language of physics or computation. The right level of analysis is neither particle physics nor external behavior; it is the level of organized perceptual appearance.

4. Qualia: the foundation of mind

Haikonen’s theory of qualia is the heart of his philosophy of mind. In standard philosophy, qualia are often treated as ineffable private qualities, the “what-it-is-like” of experience. Haikonen accepts the importance of this tradition but tries to redefine qualia in a way that can be used for engineering. In his later work, he says that existing definitions of qualia are often vague, and he proposes a more general account in which qualia are essential to the mind-body problem, consciousness, symbol grounding, and meaningful AI.

For Haikonen, qualia are not properties of the external world. Redness is not simply “out there” as an intrinsic object-property in the way mass or spatial extension might be. Nor is pain simply a physical property of tissue damage. Qualia are responses of a sensory system that manifest internally. They are how sensory information is given to the subject. A quale is not an interpreted symbol for a property; it is the primary appearing of that property-like content to the system.

This is why he calls them “self-explanatory.” A symbol such as the word “red” must be interpreted through a network of other symbols, memories, or conventions. But the appearance of red, as a qualitative presentation, does not require translation into another code in order to be what it is for the subject. Similarly, a pain signal is not first a neutral symbol that must be looked up in a semantic table. Its painfulness is already its mode of significance. Haikonen uses this to connect qualia with meaning: qualia are primitive meaning-bearing presentations.

This makes his theory strongly anti-symbolic at the foundation. Symbols can have meaning only by being grounded in non-symbolic qualitative presentations. Inner speech can be meaningful because words are associated with qualitative, sensorimotor, emotional, and bodily states. Abstract thought is possible, but it is not basic. The base layer of mind is direct qualitative presentation.

Haikonen also emphasizes the transparency of qualia. In ordinary perception, one does not experience retinal signals, neural spikes, or brain states. One sees a cup, hears a voice, feels a surface, or senses pain in one’s body. The perceptual content seems directed toward the world or the body, not toward the representational medium. He argues that this transparency is a core feature of genuine perception and a design requirement for conscious machines.

This leads to an important point: Haikonen’s qualia are not merely decorative phenomenal colors pasted onto cognition. They are the medium of direct perception and grounding. Without qualia, a system may classify, detect, compute, and respond, but it does not perceive in the conscious sense. It may have information, but that information does not appear.

5. Perception: the primary form of consciousness

Haikonen’s philosophy is perceptualist. Consciousness begins with perception, not with abstract reasoning, linguistic judgment, or self-reflective thought. The object of consciousness is first of all a perceptual object: something appearing through sensory qualities. His table of contents and chapter structure in Consciousness and Robot Sentience are revealing: he moves from the “object of consciousness” to qualia, percepts, “no qualia, no percepts,” and “no percepts—no consciousness.”

This perceptualism has several consequences.

First, consciousness is embodied. A conscious system must not merely receive detached data. It must perceive through a body-like arrangement of sensors, actions, proprioception, pain/pleasure states, motion, resistance, and environmental feedback. Percepts are stabilized and externalized through sensorimotor loops. The system learns that certain appearances change with its movement and action, and this helps locate percepts as “out there” rather than as arbitrary internal events.

Second, consciousness is multimodal. Human experience is not isolated vision, isolated hearing, or isolated tactile data. It is an integrated field of mutually informing modalities: seeing, hearing, touch, proprioception, affective tone, action readiness, and verbal association. Haikonen therefore demands information integration and sensorimotor integration, but his integration is not merely mathematical; it is qualitative and associative.

Third, consciousness requires attention and novelty detection. His architecture includes match/mismatch, novelty, sensory attention, visual searching, predictive perception, and feedback mechanisms. Conscious content is not a passive array of raw stimuli; it is organized by relevance, expectation, surprise, and action.

Fourth, perception is already meaningful. It is not first meaningless sensory data later converted into meaning by language. The feel of pain, the directionality of sound, the shape of an object, and the resistance of contact already have practical significance for the system. This is where Haikonen departs from views that locate meaning primarily in language, concepts, or formal representation. For him, meaning is rooted in qualitative sensorimotor appearance.

6. Symbol grounding and the critique of digital computation

Haikonen’s critique of ordinary AI is not simply that current systems are insufficiently advanced. It is that a whole class of systems is based on the wrong kind of informational relation. Traditional symbolic AI manipulates formal signs whose meaning depends on external interpretation. A program can process the word “red,” a token for “pain,” or a representation of “danger” without any redness, pain, or danger appearing to the system. This leaves the system semantically hollow.

His answer is not to abandon all symbols. Rather, he wants symbols to be grounded in direct qualitative content. Human language works because words are associated with perceptions, actions, emotions, and bodily states. Inner speech is meaningful because it is embedded in a network of qualitative and sensorimotor associations. A conscious robot, therefore, could use words and symbols, but only if those symbols are tied to self-explanatory perceptual appearances.

This is why Haikonen is skeptical of digital computers as ordinary symbol manipulators. He often contrasts his architecture with conventional digital, programmed, algorithmic, symbol-based computation. The XCR-1 robot, for example, is presented as a non-digital, non-programmed implementation based on associative neural principles rather than a microprocessor executing conventional pre-written algorithms.

This point must be handled carefully. Critics such as Hudlicka argue that Haikonen’s anti-computationalism may be too narrow, because it often targets a particular image of computation—binary, von Neumann, symbolic, externally interpreted programming—rather than the broader theoretical notion of computation, which can include analog, parallel, dynamical, embodied, and neural computation. Chrisley likewise argues that Haikonen overstates the claim that digital computers or symbol systems cannot have qualia, especially because symbolic mechanisms can exist at a subpersonal level without experience itself being symbolic.

The charitable reconstruction is this: Haikonen’s deepest objection is not to “computation” in every mathematically possible sense. His objection is to ungrounded formal manipulation. If a computational system merely transforms externally interpreted tokens, it is not conscious. If, however, a machine’s informational states become direct qualitative appearances integrated with perception, action, memory, self, and affect, then it may be conscious, whether one ultimately describes the implementation as computational, dynamical, neural, or hybrid. Haikonen himself prefers to describe the relevant machinery as associative and non-symbolic rather than computational in the standard AI sense.

7. Thought, inner speech, imagery, and introspection

Haikonen does not restrict consciousness to external sensory perception. He treats inner speech, imagery, and introspection as derived forms of perceptual appearance. Human thinking often occurs as quasi-auditory inner speech, quasi-visual imagery, bodily anticipation, emotional coloring, and associative recall. These are not abstract propositions floating in a mental void. They are internally evoked perceptual-like states.

This lets him explain introspection architecturally. A system can re-enter its own internally generated representations into perceptual-like processing loops. It can distinguish externally caused representations from internally evoked ones. It can report its own imaginations, inner speech, and affective states because these states are cross-connected with linguistic and motor-report systems. His doctoral architecture includes reentrant perception/response loops, inner representations, introspection through reentry, and cross-module reportability.

This is a significant part of his philosophy of mind. He does not treat introspection as a mysterious inner eye inspecting private objects. Nor does he reduce it to linguistic behavior. Instead, introspection is a system’s capacity to have internally generated contents appear in the same general qualitative medium as perception, while also marking them as internally generated.

This has affinities with higher-order and global-access theories, but with a difference. A higher-order theorist might say that consciousness involves a representation of a representation. A global workspace theorist might say that conscious content is globally available for report and control. Haikonen would accept that reportability and cross-system availability matter, but he would deny that they are sufficient. The introspected content must appear qualitatively. Without appearance, there is only access or information flow, not consciousness in the full phenomenal sense.

8. Emotion, motivation, pain, and pleasure

Haikonen’s philosophy of mind is not purely cognitive. He gives emotion, motivation, pain, and pleasure a structural role. Conscious systems must not merely identify objects; they must register significance. They must have system-level reactions that make some states attractive, aversive, urgent, disruptive, rewarding, or harmful. His architectures include emotional significance, pain/pleasure, motivation, and self-preservational reactions.

This matters for two reasons. First, affect helps ground meaning. A perceptual object is not merely shaped and colored; it may be threatening, desirable, painful, familiar, strange, useful, or forbidden. Second, selfhood is tied to bodily significance. Pain, touch, proprioception, and bodily boundaries help distinguish self from world. A robot with no bodily vulnerability or affective differentiation would be missing a major part of what makes consciousness subjectively organized.

Haikonen’s treatment of machine pain is especially revealing. He does not think pain is simply a label attached to damage detection. For a robot to have pain-like experience, the relevant state must be integrated into its qualitative, motivational, and self-related architecture. It must be something that appears to the system with aversive significance, not merely a variable named pain.

There is, however, a tension in his theory. On one reading, direct consciousness itself does not “execute” cognitive functions; rather, cognitive processes perform functions and their results become conscious. On another reading, qualia are not epiphenomenal, because without them there are no conscious percepts and no grounded meanings. The best interpretation is that qualia are not executive modules but modes of presentation: they do not replace cognitive mechanisms, but they make perception, meaning, and report phenomenally real.

9. Self-consciousness and the embodied self

Haikonen’s theory of self-consciousness is grounded in body representation. A robot’s self-concept cannot be merely a symbolic token such as “I” or a database entry containing its own name. It must be grounded in somatosensory, proprioceptive, tactile, motor, and affective experience. The system must distinguish its own body from the external world, associate sensations with its own actions, and connect self-related percepts with reportable representations.

This is why Haikonen is interested in mirror recognition and self-related tests, but he does not treat them as behaviorist proof. A robot might be programmed to pass a mirror test without self-consciousness. The important question is whether mirror-related information activates a grounded self-model based on bodily perception and self-related qualitative states.

The self, in this reconstruction, is not an immaterial ego. It is also not just a linguistic fiction. It is an integrated, embodied, qualitative control structure: a network of bodily appearances, action possibilities, proprioceptive invariants, affective significance, memories, name associations, and reportable inner states. Self-consciousness arises when these self-related contents become internally apparent and available for introspection and communication.

This gives Haikonen a position between two extremes. Against Cartesian ego theories, he does not posit a separate inner subject-substance. Against eliminativist or purely linguistic accounts, he insists that selfhood has a real experiential basis in bodily qualitative organization.

10. The Haikonen Cognitive Architecture

The practical center of Haikonen’s project is the Haikonen Cognitive Architecture, or HCA. It is meant to implement the conditions just described: direct perception, associative processing, distributed representations, multimodal integration, feedback loops, attention, inner imagery, inner speech, emotion, self-modeling, and report. Haikonen’s work repeatedly contrasts this with architectures based solely on symbolic programming or conventional artificial neural networks.

HCA uses associative neurons and groups of neurons whose signals preserve point-of-origin and meaning-like structure. It is intended to combine sub-symbolic and symbolic processing without reducing cognition to arbitrary symbol manipulation. Its perception-response loops allow sensory inputs, motor responses, expectations, and internally evoked representations to interact. These loops support match/mismatch detection, novelty, attention, imagination, and introspection.

The XCR-1 robot is Haikonen’s main implementation and demonstration platform. It is described as a simple three-wheel robot with gripper hands, sensory modalities, self-talk, associative neural modules, and cross-modal integration. The robot includes auditory and visual modules, emotional and pain/pleasure mechanisms, a self-concept, gripper and wheel control, self-talk, and cross-associative information integration.

Haikonen does not claim that a crude robot like XCR-1 has human-level consciousness. The more important claim is architectural: if consciousness depends on qualitative, associative, embodied, integrated perception rather than abstract symbol manipulation, then a robot built according to these principles is at least on the right path. Critics have noted that XCR-1’s demonstrated capacities are limited and that moving from architecture to consciousness remains philosophically and empirically difficult.

Still, the architecture clarifies Haikonen’s philosophical commitments. Consciousness is not to be added later as a module. It is supposed to arise from the way perception, action, meaning, self, affect, and report are organized.

11. Artificial consciousness: necessary conditions

Haikonen’s artificial consciousness is not a generic claim that sufficiently advanced AI will “wake up.” It has specific design requirements. A genuinely conscious artificial system must have phenomenal internal appearances; cognitive ability alone is insufficient.

The first condition is direct qualitative perception. The system must not merely classify sensory data; information must appear in qualitative form. The second is non-symbolic grounding. Symbols must be associated with self-explanatory sensory and bodily contents. The third is multimodal integration. Visual, auditory, tactile, proprioceptive, affective, linguistic, and motor states must be integrated into a common cognitive economy. The fourth is sensorimotor embodiment. Percepts must be stabilized through action, feedback, and world-coupling. The fifth is introspective reentry. Internally generated imagery, speech, and memory must become available as reportable mental content. The sixth is affective significance. Pain, pleasure, motivation, and emotional relevance must shape the system’s experience. The seventh is a grounded self-concept. The system must distinguish self from world through bodily and sensorimotor structure.

This framework makes Haikonen skeptical of many AI consciousness claims. A language model that manipulates text tokens, for example, would not be conscious by Haikonen’s standards merely because it produces fluent reports about consciousness. It would lack direct sensory qualia, bodily self-grounding, affective pain/pleasure in his sense, sensorimotor externalization, and qualitative self-explanatory meanings. That conclusion follows from Haikonen’s criteria rather than from a general proof about all possible AI. His position is also stricter than many recent indicator-based approaches, which evaluate AI systems by a family of architectural features drawn from neuroscientific theories and do not treat qualia in Haikonen’s specific self-explanatory-information sense.

12. Testing consciousness: why behavior is not enough

Haikonen is skeptical of Turing-style tests and purely behavioral tests. A system can behave as if it is conscious without having subjective appearances. It can report pain without pain, describe red without seeing red, and identify itself in a mirror without a grounded self-experience. For Haikonen, tests of artificial consciousness must therefore be indirect and architecture-sensitive. They must ask whether the system has the right internal perceptual, associative, embodied, and introspective machinery.

This does not mean that behavior is irrelevant. Reports, self-talk, novelty responses, pain behavior, mirror behavior, and sensorimotor adaptation can provide evidence. But they are not decisive by themselves. They must be interpreted in light of the system’s internal design. A scripted report is weak evidence; a report arising from grounded qualitative perception and integrated introspection is stronger evidence.

This gives Haikonen a kind of architectural epistemology of consciousness. We cannot directly observe another system’s qualia, whether biological or artificial. But we can evaluate the plausibility of consciousness by examining the mechanisms that would make qualitative appearance, grounded meaning, selfhood, and report possible. This is close in spirit to current theory-based approaches to AI consciousness, though Haikonen’s own criteria are more qualia-centered and more hostile to ungrounded digital computation.

13. A systematic philosophy of mind extracted from Haikonen

Haikonen’s work can be reconstructed as a comprehensive philosophy of mind built around the following theses.

First, appearance realism. Consciousness is real because appearances are real. A color experience, pain, sound, or inner image is not a fiction, even if it is not a material object in the external world. The basic explanandum is the subjective internal appearance of information.

Second, naturalistic anti-dualism without reductive materialism. Consciousness is not an immaterial soul, but it is also not captured by describing matter and energy alone. It is achievable by physical systems, but only at the level of organized perceptual systems.

Third, qualia primacy. Qualia are not marginal cases; they are the basic form in which sensory information becomes consciously present. Perception depends on qualia, and consciousness depends on perception.

Fourth, meaning begins in self-explanatory information. The solution to symbol grounding is not more symbols, but direct qualitative presentations that do not require translation into another code. Words and symbols become meaningful by association with such presentations.

Fifth, thought is internalized perception. Inner speech, imagery, memory, and imagination are internally evoked perceptual-like states. Conscious thinking is therefore not pure abstract calculation; it is grounded in qualitative, associative, sensorimotor content.

Sixth, embodiment is constitutive, not optional. Consciousness requires a world-facing and body-facing system. Sensorimotor loops, proprioception, tactile boundaries, pain/pleasure, and motor feedback are not peripheral add-ons; they structure objecthood, externality, and selfhood.

Seventh, integration is necessary but not sufficient. Multimodal and cognitive integration matter, but not because integration alone magically produces consciousness. Integration matters because it allows qualitative contents to be cross-associated, attended to, remembered, acted upon, and reported.

Eighth, consciousness includes affective significance. A mind is not only a classifier of objects but a system to which things matter. Pain, pleasure, motivation, novelty, and emotional significance are part of the architecture of conscious cognition.

Ninth, artificial consciousness is possible but demanding. A conscious robot is possible in principle, but not because any sufficiently fluent or complex symbol manipulator is conscious. It must possess grounded, qualitative, embodied, integrated, introspectable perceptual states.

Tenth, third-person evidence is indirect. Consciousness cannot be verified merely by behavior. The best evidence comes from combining behavior with knowledge of the system’s internal architecture.

This reconstructed philosophy can be named qualia-grounded embodied artificial phenomenalism. It is phenomenalist because appearance is central; embodied because the system must be sensorimotor and self-grounded; artificial because machines can instantiate the required architecture; and qualia-grounded because meanings and percepts begin with qualitative self-explanatory information.

14. Comparison with major positions

Compared with Cartesian dualism, Haikonen preserves the seriousness of subjective appearance but rejects a separate mental substance. Consciousness is not a ghost in the machine; it is a system-level mode of presentation in the machine.

Compared with reductive identity theory, he is more phenomenologically conservative. He does not think that identifying a neural correlate or physical substrate by itself explains why experience appears as it does. The problem is not just correlation between brain states and reports; it is the internal appearance of information.

Compared with functionalism, Haikonen accepts architecture and causal organization but denies that externally described function is sufficient. Function without qualia is as-if consciousness, not real consciousness.

Compared with global workspace theory, Haikonen shares an interest in integration, broadcast-like availability, reportability, and cognitive access, but he thinks such theories risk explaining access consciousness while leaving phenomenal appearance untouched. Reviewers note that he explicitly compares his architecture with Baars and Shanahan while maintaining that machine consciousness must address phenomenal consciousness, not just access and coordination.

Compared with Searle, Haikonen agrees that syntax is not enough and that symbol manipulation alone does not yield understanding. But he rejects the pessimistic conclusion that machines cannot be conscious. Properly built machines can have qualitative grounding.

Compared with Harnad, Haikonen radicalizes symbol grounding by making qualia central. Grounding is not just sensorimotor connection; it is direct self-explanatory qualitative presentation.

Compared with embodied and enactive approaches, Haikonen agrees that perception and selfhood depend on action, bodily feedback, and environment-coupling. But he remains more internalist than many enactivists, because he defines consciousness as internal subjective appearance of information. The world and action are essential to forming appearances, but the conscious event remains an internal appearing.

Compared with integrated information theory, Haikonen shares the thought that integration matters, but he does not define consciousness abstractly as integrated information. His emphasis is on qualitative, perceptual, self-explanatory, sensorimotor information rather than on a formal measure of integrated causal structure.

Compared with contemporary AI-consciousness indicator approaches, Haikonen is both anticipatory and idiosyncratic. He anticipates the idea that architecture matters more than behavior alone. But he is more insistent that consciousness requires qualia and grounding, and he is more skeptical of ordinary digital systems. Recent reports evaluate AI consciousness by multiple neuroscientific theories and conclude that there are no obvious technical barriers in principle, while also finding no strong evidence that current AI systems are conscious.

15. Strengths of Haikonen’s position

The first strength is that Haikonen does not evade the hard problem. Much machine-consciousness work focuses on access, report, self-monitoring, or task performance. Haikonen insists that the real problem is phenomenal appearance: how internal activity comes to be present as experience. Even critics acknowledge that he squarely addresses phenomenal consciousness rather than merely functional consciousness.

The second strength is his integration of philosophy and engineering. He does not leave qualia as a purely metaphysical mystery. He asks what kind of architecture would be needed for direct perception, grounded meaning, self-report, inner speech, pain, pleasure, and selfhood. Reviewers have noted that his work bridges philosophical discourse and practical engineering in an unusually concrete way.

The third strength is his treatment of symbol grounding. By linking grounding to self-explanatory qualitative information, he gives a unified account of semantics and phenomenology. Meaning is not merely a relation among symbols; it begins in direct qualitative contact with world and body.

The fourth strength is his attention to embodiment and affect. A conscious machine, for Haikonen, cannot be a detached theorem-prover. It must have perception, action, bodily self-modeling, pain/pleasure, motivation, and emotional significance. This makes his theory more psychologically plausible than purely linguistic or purely formal accounts.

The fifth strength is his refusal to equate simulation with instantiation. A machine that simulates pain-talk is not necessarily in pain. A machine that simulates self-recognition is not necessarily self-conscious. This distinction remains important in contemporary AI debates, especially when systems can produce increasingly convincing verbal reports without clear evidence of embodied phenomenal grounding.

16. Weaknesses and unresolved problems

The first weakness is that “self-explanatory information” may not fully explain consciousness. It identifies the kind of information that appears meaningful from the inside, but the critic can still ask: why does this information appear at all? Why does an associative sensory process produce experience rather than unconscious processing? Chrisley argues that Haikonen’s account does not fully answer the question of experiential appearance and needs a more detailed theory of experiential content and reference.

The second weakness is possible circularity. Haikonen sometimes seems to say that qualia explain consciousness and that qualia are the forms of information that appear consciously. If qualia are defined by conscious appearance, then using qualia to explain consciousness risks restating the phenomenon. Haikonen’s architecture reduces this risk by giving design constraints, but the metaphysical worry remains.

The third weakness is his anti-computational rhetoric. Critics argue that he sometimes attacks a narrow model of digital symbolic computation rather than computation as such. Modern computationalism can include distributed, probabilistic, analog, dynamical, recurrent, embodied, and neuromorphic systems. If so, Haikonen’s strongest claim should be narrowed: not “computation cannot be conscious,” but “ungrounded symbol manipulation cannot be conscious.”

The fourth weakness is the transparency problem. Haikonen emphasizes that conscious perception is direct and transparent, but critics argue that he does not fully explain how internal informational vehicles refer to external objects while disappearing from experience. The theory needs a more precise account of content, reference, and world-directedness.

The fifth weakness is developmental and social underdevelopment. Human consciousness is not formed only by architecture; it develops through infancy, social interaction, language, culture, imitation, attachment, and education. Reviewers have noted questions about whether Haikonen’s framework sufficiently addresses developmental and social dimensions.

The sixth weakness is empirical underdetermination. XCR-1 and related architectures can demonstrate associative processing, self-talk, sensorimotor loops, and basic grounded behaviors, but these demonstrations do not prove consciousness. Haikonen knows behavior alone is insufficient, but the same point applies to his own machines: architecture plus behavior still leaves room for doubt. Hudlicka notes that the robot’s capabilities are limited and that the book sometimes moves too quickly from architectural proposal to claims about solving the hard problem.

The seventh weakness is that Haikonen may underestimate the complexity of human consciousness. If human consciousness is treated mainly as a flow of percepts, qualia, inner speech, and imagery, one might miss the role of temporality, narrative identity, social cognition, conceptual abstraction, unconscious affective regulation, metacognition, and norm-governed rationality. Haikonen’s theory captures an important stratum of mind, but it may not exhaust the full human case.

17. Final assessment: Haikonen’s place in the field

Haikonen’s philosophical importance lies in the fact that he refuses two easy answers. He refuses the easy computationalist answer that sufficient functional behavior or symbol processing is consciousness. He also refuses the easy anti-AI answer that machines can never be conscious. His position is demanding but not mystical: machines can be conscious, but only if they have the right kind of qualitative, embodied, grounded, associative, integrated, self-related perceptual life.

In the larger history of philosophy of mind, he is best placed after the functionalist and computationalist era, in the period when qualia, the hard problem, embodiment, and artificial consciousness converge. He is a descendant of the qualia debate, a respondent to Searle and Harnad, a critic of disembodied symbolic AI, a partial ally of embodied cognition, and a heterodox contributor to machine consciousness. His distinctive contribution is to insist that artificial consciousness must be designed around phenomenal appearance, not merely intelligence, behavior, information processing, or report.

The most defensible version of his philosophy is not the strongest claim that he has solved the hard problem. The defensible version is this: Haikonen provides a rigorous engineering-philosophical research program for identifying what artificial systems would need before claims of consciousness become serious. Those requirements include direct qualitative perception, grounded meaning, inner imagery, inner speech, sensorimotor embodiment, affective significance, self-modeling, introspective access, and integrated report. That program remains valuable even where his metaphysical explanation is incomplete.

If one had to condense the extracted philosophy of mind into a single formula, it would be:

Mind is the organized internal appearance of meaning-bearing information in an embodied perceptual system; consciousness is the flow of such appearances as qualia, images, feelings, inner speech, and self-related bodily states; artificial consciousness is possible when a machine’s architecture generates these appearances directly rather than merely simulating their reports.

PreviousIgor Aleksander NextOwen Holland

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
